methodology_sections: 1. Conservation Laws - Solvency, inflation checks, supply integrity
2. Relationship & Ratio Invariants - Collateralization, rate consistency
3. Economic Monotonicity - Yield accumulation, exchange rate direction
4. Incentive & Game Theoretic Integrity - Attack cost vs profit, slashing
5. Fee & Yield Consistency - Fee accounting, yield distribution

invariants.description: The PSM's internal accounting of peg tokens must never exceed its actual peg token balance.
invariants.attack_scenario: If pegTokenBalance exceeds the actual balance, the PSM is insolvent. Users will not be able to redeem their CREDIT for peg tokens, leading to a bank run and loss of peg.

invariants.description: The ProfitManager's CREDIT token balance must fully back all surplus buffers.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the ProfitManager's CREDIT balance is less than the recorded surplus buffers, the protocol is insolvent regarding its first-loss capital. Losses would revert when trying to burn the buffer, breaking the bad debt socialization mechanism.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_surplus_buffer_backing() public view { assert(CreditToken(credit).balanceOf(address(this)) >= surplusBuffer); /* plus term buffers */ }

invariants.description: The term's issuance must exactly equal the sum of the borrowAmount of all active loans.
invariants.attack_scenario: If issuance is artificially lower than the actual active debt, the term could bypass its debtCeiling or hardCap, leading to over-borrowing and systemic risk. If higher, it could DOS new borrows.
invariants.invariant_logic: issuance == sum(loans[id].borrowAmount for all id where loans[id].closeTime == 0)

invariants.description: The creditMultiplier must monotonically decrease or remain constant; it can never increase.
invariants.attack_scenario: If an attacker can manipulate the creditMultiplier to increase, they could artificially inflate the value of CREDIT, allowing them to drain the PSM of peg tokens or borrow more than allowed.

invariants.description: The rebasing share price must monotonically increase or remain constant; it can never decrease.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the share price decreases, rebasing users would lose a portion of their principal/yield, effectively stealing funds from lenders.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_share_price_monotonicity() public view { assert(rebasingSharePrice() >= oldRebasingSharePrice); }

invariants.description: The total supply must equal the sum of the non-rebasing supply and the rebasing supply.
invariants.attack_scenario: If this conservation law is broken, the token contract is inflating or deflating unexpectedly, which could allow attackers to mint unbacked tokens or destroy user balances.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_supply_conservation() public view { assert(totalSupply() == nonRebasingSupply() + rebasingSupply()); }

invariants.description: During an auction settlement, the sum of collateral sent to the borrower and the bidder must exactly equal the loan's locked collateral amount.
invariants.attack_scenario: If collateral is leaked or locked during an auction, either the borrower is robbed of their excess collateral, or the bidder gets free collateral, draining the lending term.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_auction_collateral_conservation(bytes32 loanId, uint256 collateralToBorrower, uint256 collateralToBidder) public view { assert(collateralToBorrower + collateralToBidder == loans[loanId].collateralAmount || (collateralToBorrower == 0 && collateralToBidder == 0)); }
